title: Do mTOR, IGF 1 and dairy accelerate aging?

chunks.title: Introduction to IGF-1 and Dairy
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses IGF-1 levels and dairy's role in nutrition, challenging common perceptions about dairy activating IGF-1. They assert that their own IGF-1 levels remain low despite a diet rich in meat, organs, and dairy.
chunks.transcript: regard to igf-1 insulin-like growth factor one I'm going to do a little segment here on Dairy in a moment but Dairy has often been vilified for activating igf-1 but I would challenge vegan and vegetarians to show me what their levels of igf-1 are I've shown my blood work multiple times and my igf-1 remains below the average of the human population Often by half or a full standard deviation so here I am a 45 year old male who is exercising not fearing mtor with my exercise who's getting out in the sun who's eating a lot of meat and organs either fresh or desiccated like I get from hardened soil I'm eating fruit and honey and maple syrup and a lot of carbohydrates and a lot of dairy my igf-1 should be through

chunks.title: Critique of Dairy-Related Mortality Claims
chunks.summary: The speaker critiques studies linking dairy consumption to aging and mortality, arguing that many claims are based on flawed assumptions and overlooking contradicting research. They highlight the Maasai people's health as a counterexample to negative views on dairy.

chunks.title: Full-Fat Dairy Benefits
chunks.summary: Evidence suggests that high-fat dairy consumption is inversely related to obesity risk and may have beneficial effects on health. The speaker encourages the consumption of full-fat dairy products, citing their personal dietary habits and blood work.
chunks.transcript: eat your full fat Dairy eat your raw whole milk and eat your cheese so again milk haters beware I am coming for you there's also plenty of good evidence that kids who drink full fat milk are at less of a risk of obesity so very clear pattern that appears to be beneficial for humans with dairy and again I've had a lot of dairy in my diet recently and I've shown my blood work I would challenge vegans and vegetarians to show me that their igf-1 is lower than mine so any podcasts looking to debunk plant-based arguments and Longevity

chunks.title: Low Protein Diets and Longevity Research
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses a study suggesting low protein diets reduce cancer risk and mortality among those under 65, while highlighting biases in the findings. They argue that protein becomes increasingly important for longevity as individuals age.
chunks.transcript: arguments would be incomplete without consideration of this paper from Walter Longo it is an epidemiology study claiming that low protein intake is associated with a major reduction in igf-1 cancer and overall mortality in the 65 and younger but not older population if we just pause for a moment is it possible that there's some healthy user bias here or some unhealthy user bias here because I believe what's happening in the study is that there is healthy user bias and unhealthy user bias meaning that people are reading more protein are doing more unhealthy behaviors it is impossible to tease these two things out and the people that are eating less protein are doing more healthy behaviors and that is why we see a reduction in cancer and mortality less than 65. but I also believe that meat and protein is so important for the human organism then once we get past 65 the unhelp user bias and the health user bias are completely wiped off the map and even these researchers are forced to admit that

chunks.title: Criticism of Epidemiological Studies
chunks.summary: The speaker criticizes the reliability of epidemiological studies, particularly those on margarine and heart disease, emphasizing unhealthy user bias influencing results. They share the complicated relationship between diet and heart disease outcomes.

chunks.title: Benefits and Risks of mTOR
chunks.summary: The speaker addresses the implications of moderating mTOR activation, warning against its potential immunological trade-offs. They stress the need for adequate protein signals from the diet to support immune function.
chunks.transcript: brownies with refined flowers so so how many people do you know that are Rebels that don't listen to health advice that just eat meat with nothing else no junk food and again I'm talking about mainstream westernized Americans not health conscious people making a distinct choice to be a carnivore or animal-based diet and as we mentioned earlier mtor not something that we necessarily want to avoid triggering not something that we can avoid triggering if we're going to eat any food or do any sort of movement and then these longevity proponents never discuss that there is an immunological trade-off to an mtor deficiency that if you limit mtor or if you knock out mtor or if someone developed a drug that blocks mtor like rapamycin there are immunologic trade-offs leading to increased infections with microbacterial infections like tuberculosis so I have major concerns that this rapamycin craze will lead to increased tuberculosis increased pulmonary infections increase infections overall because guess what if you don't tell your body that you're thriving if you don't give your body an abundance signal with protein specifically the amino acid leucine carbohydrates the postprandial insulin signal your immune system may suffer why would we think that we're smarter than hundreds of thousands of years of evolution and that we should do something different that our ancestors have done for hundreds of thousands of years because they look pretty healthy to me

chunks.title: Human Populations and Animal-Based Diets
chunks.summary: The speaker highlights healthy human populations that thrive on animal-based diets, free from chronic diseases. They argue that incorporating insights from anthropology into medicine could challenge prevailing dietary misconceptions.
chunks.transcript: and Western medicine is completely ignorant to this I don't understand why anthropology is not taught in medical schools that would I think create so much dissension among the medical students when they realize that there are still free living populations of humans on this Earth that are free from chronic diseases as we know them today that are ramped in our population there are humans living on this Earth that are free from obesity that are free from diabetes that are free from cardiovascular disease that are free from autoimmunity cancer depression dementia Etc and guess what these humans eat meat and organs and lots of animal fat and a good amount of carbohydrates because they'll eat honey anytime they can get it I'm talking about the hods of the akong the yanamono and Brazil so many populations around the globe illustrate this is a cognitive distance that would explode many the mind of a medical
